summary: Julia Evans documents her journey to solve a seemingly simple concurrency problem: submitting work to a thread pool with backpressure so it blocks when overwhelmed, like Unix pipes. After discovering that Java's standard library doesn't offer this directly, she finds an elegant solution in the book 'Java Concurrency in Practice' using a semaphore-based BoundedExecutor pattern. The post captures her excitement at discovering semaphores as a concurrency primitive and celebrates the satisfaction of finally getting parallelization working correctly.
key_points: Unix pipes provide an ideal concurrency model: an internal buffer that blocks the producer when full, preventing overload
Java's standard library surprisingly lacks a built-in way to submit to a thread pool with blocking when the pool is busy
A semaphore-based BoundedExecutor from 'Java Concurrency in Practice' elegantly solves the backpressure problem by blocking submissions when workers are all busy
Semaphores are a powerful concurrency primitive: a shared counter where acquire() blocks at zero until someone calls release()
Python's ThreadPoolExecutor is even more limited, not allowing configuration of the internal submission queue
money_quotes: I wanted to send a bunch of work to some worker threads, in parallel, have them do the work without anything exploding. That's it.
I thought a bit about how Unix pipes are one of my favorite abstractions. You pipe stuff from thing1 to thing2, it works, nothing explodes.
Pipes have an internal buffer, and when the buffer is full, thing1 is not allowed to write to the pipe any more. It blocks.
So, is there something in Java's standard library that lets you submit to a thread pool and block if the thread pool is too busy? Spoiler: no, there is not.
I didn't know what a semaphore was until I read this code and I was like OH THIS IS AMAZING AND SO USEFUL AND WOW.

A semaphore is just a shared int that you can decrement and increment! acquire() decrements, release() increments. And if it gets to 0, semaphore.acquire() will block until someone else has released it.
key_insight: The best concurrency model mirrors Unix pipes — a bounded buffer with backpressure — and a simple semaphore wrapper around a thread pool achieves exactly that.
